coredata.dc:title: Quantum dots: A new approach in thermodynamic inhibitor for the drilling of gas hydrate bearing formation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Saikia, Tinku
Mahto, Vikas
Kumar, Amit
coredata.dc:description: The large amount of thermodynamic hydrate inhibitor is required for hydrate inhibition in drilling fluid and causes environmental problem and proved to be uneconomical during deep well drilling. Quantum dots may be the solution for these problems. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) prepared from ethylene glycol and hydrate inhibition efficiency of CDs were compared with ethylene glycol. THF (Tetrahydrofuran) hydrate was used for the testing of hydrate inhibition efficiency at 1°C. The CDs do not affect the drilling fluid rheology adversely and inhibits the hydrate crystals better than EG. Hence, CDs can be effectively used in drilling fluid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Drilling fluid
Clathrate hydrate crystal
Rheological parameters
Carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup for clathrate hydrate induction studies.

UV–vis absorption of the CDs in aqueous solution; inset image shows digital photographs of CDs (A) under visible light (B) under UV light (365nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).
Photoluminescence spectra of one pot synthesized ethylene glycol CDs at different excitations.
HRTEM image of CDs formed; inset image shows corresponding size distribution of the CDs.
FTIR spectra of ethylene glycol and ethylene glycol CDs.
Increase in temperature of experimental solution (0.1wt% EG & CDs) indicative of clathrate hydrate induction.
Increase in temperature of experimental solution (0.3wt% EG & CDs) indicative of clathrate hydrate induction.
Increase in temperature of experimental solution (0.5wt% EG & CDs) indicative of clathrate hydrate induction.
Increase in temperature of experimental solution (0.7wt% EG & CDs) indicative of clathrate hydrate induction.
Increase in temperature of experimental solution (1.0wt% EG & CDs) indicative of clathrate hydrate induction.
Clathrate hydrate induction time in different experimental solution.
Effect of ethylene glycol on the rheology of drilling fluid.
Effect of carbon dots on the rheology of drilling fluid.
Most preferable drilling fluid system with CDs and comparison with EG drilling fluid.

head.ce:title: Quantum dots: A new approach in thermodynamic inhibitor for the drilling of gas hydrate bearing formation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Petroleum Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad 826004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines), Dhanbad 826004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Technology (Indian School of Mines)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The large amount of thermodynamic hydrate inhibitor is required for hydrate inhibition in drilling fluid and causes environmental problem and proved to be uneconomical during deep well drilling. Quantum dots may be the solution for these problems. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) prepared from ethylene glycol and hydrate inhibition efficiency of CDs were compared with ethylene glycol. THF (Tetrahydrofuran) hydrate was used for the testing of hydrate inhibition efficiency at 1°C. The CDs do not affect the drilling fluid rheology adversely and inhibits the hydrate crystals better than EG. Hence, CDs can be effectively used in drilling fluid.

ce:section.ce:para: During deep water drilling the pressure on the seabed usually vary in between several hundreds of bars and temperature ranges from −2°C to +4°C, depending upon the geographical location [1]. The conditions of low temperature and high pressure found during deep water drilling favors the formation of gas hydrates in the drilling fluid. Gas hydrate, an ice-like solid has a stable clathrate structure, in which gas molecules are trapped in the cage like structure of water molecules [2]. The gas hydrate stability zone extends from 150m to 1100m in permafrost. In the deep sea, the hydrate stability zone extends from 300m to 2000m below the ocean floor [3]. The presence of hydrates in the drilling fluid changes the rheology of drilling fluid and may lead to hydrate plugging. These hydrate plugs formed in drilling fluid flow lines and risk the operational safety [4]. The drilling accidents took place in the Gulf of Mexico and Santa Barbara, California was because of gas hydrate formation in drilling fluid [5]. To prevent the formation of hydrates in the drilling fluid, hydrate inhibitors was developed in due course of time. Hydrate inhibitors were broadly classified as thermodynamic inhibitors and low-dosage hydrate inhibitor (LDHIs) [6]. Thermodynamic inhibitors shift the dissociation/stability curve outside the hydrate stability zone, but LDHIs only delays the hydrate nucleation and growth of hydrate crystal [7]. LDHIs are again classified as Kinetic hydrate inhibitors (KHIs) and anti-agglomerants (AAs). Ethylene glycol, propylene glycol, glycerols and salts (e.g., sodium chloride) are some of the thermodynamic inhibitors used. Blends of salt and glycols have strong hydrate inhibition efficiency, but, their use was limited. The amount of salt used in drilling fluid depends upon the saturation and density of the drilling fluid. Large amounts of glycols are required in drilling fluid for effective hydrate inhibition and this will increases the drilling cost [8]. Moreover, solubility of salts decreases with increase in concentration of glycols [1]. Kinetic hydrate inhibitors are economical as it was used in smaller amounts, i.e. <1wt %. But, it only delays the hydrate nucleation and do not shift the stability/dissociation curve outside the hydrate stability zone. For the time period during which kinetic inhibitor delays the nucleation and growth of hydrate crystals depends on the rate of subcooling in the system [9].

The main disadvantage associated with thermodynamic inhibitor was that the large amount is required in drilling fluid for effective hydrate inhibition. But if somehow the thermodynamic inhibitor shows good inhibition efficiency at lower concentrations, they will become the most effective hydrate inhibitors. Quantum dot may become the answer to this problem. Quantum dots are the materials which have all the three dimensions in the nanoscale and these molecular size nanocrystals are smaller than 10nm in size [10]. This causes an increase in the relative surface area of the material and quantum effects. Because of strong quantum confinement effect, quantum dots show photoelectric properties such as low photo-bleaching, wavelength and size dependent luminance, etc. [11]. Carbon dots have excellent fluorescence properties and show better water solubility, biocompatibility and low toxicity [12]. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) possess special optical properties; it shows not only the normal or down-converted photoluminescence (PL) but also up-converted photoluminescence [13,14]. Moreover, CDs also show PL in the near infrared spectral region [15]. Quantum dots are synthesized using different methods, which are broadly classified as bottom-up and top-down synthesis processes. Bottom-up synthesis process was again subdivided into wet chemical methods and vapor phase method [16]. Different research articles developed CDs from different sources and present their effective use in different fields. Kang et al., [10] presents the use of carbon quantum dots for zebrafish fluorescence imaging. Zhang et al., [17] derives carbon quantum dots from egg whites. Wang et al., [18] shows the use of quantum dots in the sensing of Iron(III) and Escherichia coli. But, no research was conducted for the use of CDs in drilling fluid for hydrate inhibition.

This research paper for the first time presents the use of CDs in drilling fluid. An experimental study was conducted to check the effectiveness of ethylene glycol CDs in comparison to ethylene glycol to inhibit the hydrate formation in drilling fluid. The effect of CDs on the rheology of drilling fluid was also studied at a temperature of 25°C, 20°C, 10°C and 1°C, representing the conditions from surface to subsurface of deep sea [19].

Ethylene glycol quantum dots/CDs were formed using ethylene glycol purchased from LOBA Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. THF used for hydrate formation was purchased from Merck Specialities Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. Polyanionic cellulose (PAC), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), potassium chloride (KCl) and xantham gum was used for drilling fluid preparation and were purchased from Central Drug House Pvt. Ltd. New Delhi, India.

Carbon Dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of ethylene glycol in Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The ethylene glycol was subjected to constant temperature of 180°C for 6h in the autoclave. The autoclave was allowed to cool down naturally after the reaction. The synthesized CDs are then centrifuged and filtered to remove any large particles present in the solution and further purification of CDs is done by dialyzing for about 24h. The CDs are stored below 4°C for future use.

To check and compare the functional groups of carbon dots, the FTIR investigation was done using PerkinElmer “Spectrum Two FT-IR Spectrometer” from 400 to 4000cm−1. The size of the CDs was studied with the help of high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) using Phillips CM 200 transmission electron microscope. PerkinElmer LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer was used to check the characteristic emission spectra of CDs. The digital photographic image of CDs in normal light as well as in UV light of 365nm was also compared to check the fluorescence of the CDs. The CDs showed optical absorption in the UV region and this absorption of CDs was measured using Perkin Elmer 955, USA.

To test the effectiveness of hydrate inhibitors different setups were used, such as rocking cells, rotating wheel loops without pumps, stirred autoclaves and flow loops with pumps etc. But high pressure conditions and use of highly flammable gases for the experiment in these setups make them very risky to be used in laboratories. To solve the experimental complexities like this, tetrahydrofuran (THF) hydrates are used to test the effectivity of hydrate inhibitors in this research paper. THF hydrate are suitable for the hydrate inhibitors test as they were formed at atmospheric pressure and temperature below 4.4°C [20,21]. THF hydrate is similar to methane hydrates in almost every aspect [22]. Hydrate formation in THF solution took place in complete solution, unlike different setups where hydrate formation starts at liquid–gas junction, which prevents further mass transfer and growth of clathrate hydrates [23]. The effectiveness of the CDs in the inhibition of clathrate hydrates in the experimental solution was measured by measuring the induction time of hydrates. Induction time is the time required for the formation of hydrate clusters of visible size in the experimental solution.

To study and compare the hydrate inhibition efficiency of CDs, all the experiments are conducted in a self fabricated setup shown in Fig. 1 . The setup consists of a 500ml glass beaker, which is properly jacketed for temperature control. The coolant used for temperature control is a mixture of distilled water and 20wt% ethylene glycol. The coolant is circulated through the glass beakers jacket with the help of a circulation pump. The experimental solution of 100ml was placed in the beaker, which is a mixture of 75ml distilled water and 25ml THF. The experimental solution was stirred at 100rpm using a magnetic stirrer to reduce the mass and heat transfer resistance and create a homogeneous environment in the solution [24–26]. Stirring had no effect on the THF hydrate nucleation [27]. The temperature of the experimental solution was maintained at 1°C, which is lower than the equivalent temperature of 4.4°C required for THF hydrate formation.

The induction time of clathrate hydrate in the experimental solution was measured using a simplified technique. In this technique, the detection of hydrate formation in the experimental solution was visually confirmed using a Logitech C521 webcam. The visual detection technique proved to be a good technique for fast screening of new clathrate hydrate inhibitors. Different researches such as Maeda et al., [28] and Zeng et al., [29] propose and use visual techniques for identifying the formation of hydrates in their research work. But, for precise measurement of hydrates induction time in the experimental solution a digital temperature probe was used and it was found that the sudden increase in the temperature of experimental solution indicates towards the formation of the clathrate hydrates. All the experiments for measurement of hydrate induction time were repeated thrice and the average value was taken as the final induction time in a particular concentration of inhibitor.

Using CMC, PAC, xanthan gum and potassium chloride a water based drilling fluid was prepared. 0.4wt% concentration of CMC, PAC and xanthan gum is used in drilling fluid. The concentration of potassium chloride was maintained at 5.0wt%. Potassium chloride was added in drilling fluid to provide clay inhibition. The rheology of water based drilling fluid enhanced with different concentration of CDs and ethylene glycol was studied with the help of OFITE 8 speed Viscometer. During measurement of all the rheological parameters a control shear rate was maintained. In the Viscometer, constant shear rate was maintained under changing input power and fluid shear conditions by maintaining the motor RPM automatically adjusted by the OFITE Pulse-Power electronic speed regulator. A specially modified cup with a circulation chamber for temperature control was used to contain the drilling fluid during rheological studies. The temperature of drilling fluid was varied as 25°C, 20°C, 10°C and 1°C. The temperature range for the rheological study of drilling fluid was selected depending upon the temperature variation form surface to subsurface in deep sea [30]. To check the filtration properties of drilling fluid, Fann API filter press was used. American Petroleum Institute (API) recommended formulas for calculation of rheological parameters of drilling fluid are as follows:
Plastic Viscosity=Ø600 −Ø300 (cP)
Apparent Viscosity=Ø600/2 (cP)
Yield Point=Ø300 −Plastic Viscosity (lb/100ft2)

The CDs prepared from ethylene glycol by the hydrothermal treatment method was brownish in color shown in Fig. 2 (inset). To ensure that desired CDs are formed, different characteristics of carbon dots were studied such as absorbance, photolumiscence, particle size and fluorescence. The CDs were added in the experimental solution of THF and water mixture in which low temperature condition was maintained. Then, the induction time of hydrates in the solution was measured. The observed induction time of hydrates in the experimental solution with CDs was compared with the induction time of hydrates in ethylene glycol solution. The comparative study between the rheology of drilling fluid enhanced with different concentration of ethylene glycol and CDs was also conducted. The effect of the CDs on the filtration properties of drilling fluid was also studied and compared with ethylene glycol.

Optical properties of CDs were studied and found that the CDs suspension is transparent and light brown in color in visible light. Under the excitation of 365nm UV light, the CDs suspension exhibits strong blue emission shown in Fig. 2 (Inset). UV–vis spectroscopy of CDs in Fig. 2 shows two absorption peaks at 220nm and 280nm. The 220nm sharp peak is due to σ–σ* electronic transition of C–C presence and the 280nm broad peak is due to n–σ* electronic transition of C-O presence on the carbon dot surface.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra represents excitation wavelength dependent emission and with the increase in excitation wavelength the emission intensity changes. CDs PL spectra were performed at different excitation and represented in Fig. 3 . From this figure, it was clear that the CDs exhibit sharp emission spectra dependent excitations. From the emission spectrum of ethylene glycol CDs, it was seen that when CDs are excited at 400nm then the maximum emission peak was centered at 450nm. The PL properties of the ethylene glycol CDs which depends upon the excitation wavelength is may be because of different size of CDs generated and different emission spectrum.

To determine the actual size and shape of the CDs TEM analysis was done. TEM images of CDs and particle size distribution of CDs are shown in Fig. 4 . From this image, it was clear that the synthesized CDs are of spherical shape and they are well dispersed. It can be seen that the diameters of the CDs are in the range of 1–5nm. CDs are of uniform size with an average size of 3.5nm.

Fig. 5 shows FT-IR spectrum of CDs, in which strong absorption peak was shown at 3368cm−1 due to –OH group of ethylene glycol. The peaks at 2930cm−1 and 2877cm−1 are due to C–H stretching of aliphatic carbon compounds. The 1204cm−1 absorption peak is due to C–C stretching of the aliphatic carbon compound. After the formation of CDs from ethylene glycol, there is no change in absorption peaks.

Experimental solution was initially clear and transparent. The temperature of the solution was decreased to the desired temperature of 1°C. The equilibrium temperature of THF hydrate formation is 4.4°C therefore, the induction time of clathrate hydrate in the experimental solution was measured from the point when the temperature of the solution falls below 4.4°C. A temperature probe is used to measure the real time temperature of experimental solution. During the experiments, it was seen that the clear and transparent experimental solution became cloudy and translucent with time. This change in solution represents the initial stages of hydrate crystal formation. During this stage it was also seen that the temperature of the experimental solution increases suddenly from the stabilized temperature of approximately 1°C, shown in Figs. 6–10
. This sudden increase in temperature identifies the exact time at which the induction of hydrates took place in the solution and this was also visually confirmed. Induction time of hydrate crystals in the experimental solution with different concentration of hydrate inhibitors is shown in Table 1 . The experimental solution with no hydrate inhibitor gives only 7min of induction time. The higher concentration of thermodynamic inhibitor is required for efficient inhibition of clathrate hydrate. Hence, only a few minutes of induction time were obtained with a lower concentration of ethylene glycol in the experimental solution. With a concentration of 0.1wt% ethylene glycol in the experimental solution the induction time of hydrate crystal in the experimental solution only increases to 8min. Even 1.0wt% of ethylene glycol in the experimental solution only stops the induction of hydrates upto 35min. At the higher concentration range, the maximum concentration of ethylene glycol used was 5.0wt% in the experimental solution and increases the induction time to 69min.

When ethylene glycol CDs was used for hydrate inhibition in the experimental solution, then very promising results were seen even at lower concentrations. The maximum concentration of CDs used upto 1.0wt% in the experimental solution. A smaller concentration of about 0.1wt% of CDs in the solution increases the induction time to 37min, which is very much higher than 0.1wt% ethylene glycol induction time of 8min. All the concentration of CDs in the experimental solution increases the induction time of hydrates far higher than the respective ethylene glycol concentration. 1.0wt% of CDs increases the induction time of hydrates upto 110min. The ethylene glycol CDs showed enhanced hydrate inhibition efficiency at lower concentrations, similar to kinetic inhibitors and the reason for the enhanced activity in comparison to ethylene glycol (thermodynamic inhibitor) may be because of the increased relative surface area. Still, further study will be required to properly understand the mechanism involved.

In deep well drilling, the rheology was most affected by temperature in comparison to pressure [31]. The temperature range of rheological study was selected to depict the temperature variation in offshore from surface (25–30°C) to bottom (1–2°C) [32,30]. The variation in rheological parameters of drilling fluid at ambient pressure and temperatures of about 25°C, 20°C, 10°C & 1°C was studied. The rheological parameters of the drilling fluid are plastic viscosity, apparent viscosity, yield point and gel strength [33]. Filtration loss of the drilling fluid with different concentration of hydrate inhibitor was measured and found to be around 10ml in 30min. Drilling fluids gel strength also remains approximately same for all the concentrations of ethylene glycol and CDs i.e. 10s gel strength was 7lb/100ft2 and 10min gel strength was 13lb/100ft2. The effect of CDs on the rheological parameters were studied and compared with drilling fluid having ethylene glycol as hydrate inhibitor. For faster drilling operation, the lower plastic viscosity was desirable. Plastic viscosity and mud weight of the drilling fluid are directly proportional to each other. The plastic viscosity (PV) of drilling fluid without any hydrate inhibitor (base fluid) was found to be 14cP at 25°C which increases to 18.0cP at 1.0°C. Both the hydrate inhibitor, i.e. ethylene glycol and CDs show very marginal effect on the plastic viscosity of drilling fluid as shown in Tables 2 and 3
. In the drilling fluid with 1.0wt% CDs as hydrate inhibitor the PV varies from 15cP to 19cP at a temperature of 25°C and 1.0°C respectively. The higher yield point was desirable for effective removal of cuttings from the well bore. But an increase in the yield point of drilling fluid also increases the required pump pressure. Therefore a balanced yield point was desirable. The yield point of base fluid varies from 16lb/100ft2 to 25lb/100ft2 as the temperature drops from 25°C to 1.0°C. And the base fluid enhanced with 5.0wt% ethylene glycol only varied the yield point from 17lb/100ft2 to 26lb./100ft2 at 25°C and 1.0°C respectively. And CDs also showed minimum variation, at a temperature of 25°C YP found to be 17lb./100ft2 and at 1.0°C it was 26lb./100ft2. In general the apparent viscosity of drilling fluid approximately around 30cP gives a maximum penetration rate. The base fluid apparent viscosity at 25°C was 22.00cP and increases to 31.50 at a lower temperature of 1.0°C. The CDs affects the base fluids apparent viscosity marginally, at 25°C the AV found to be 23.50cP which increases to 32.50cP at 1.0°C.

From the rheological data it was clear that at lower concentration the ethylene glycol showed no considerable effect on the rheology of drilling fluid. The considerable variation in the rheological characteristic of drilling fluid was mainly because of the temperature drop. Overall, CDs showed a marginal effect on the rheological characteristics of drilling fluid in comparison to ethylene glycol. Hence CDs have proven to be effective as a clathrate hydrate inhibitor and can be used in drilling fluid.

The increase in hydrate induction time at different concentration of CDs and ethylene glycol was calculated and it was found that 0.3wt% CD was most favorable concentration for obtaining the maximum increase in induction time of hydrates. In Table 4 , the rheological properties, filtration properties and induction time of favorable CDs drilling fluid system was shown and compared with respective EG drilling fluid.

The CDs were formed from ethylene glycol and there clathrate hydrate inhibition efficiency was tested. The effect of CDs on the rheology of drilling fluid was also studied. The CDs experimental results were compared with ethylene glycol. Following conclusion are made from the studies conducted:
1.
Ethylene glycol was not much effective as a clathrate hydrate inhibitor if used in lower concentrations.
2.
CDs showed enhanced clathrate hydrate inhibition efficiency at lower concentration, below 1.0wt%.
3.
In between the concentration of hydrate inhibitor and temperature, the prominent factor which effects the rheology of drilling fluid is temperature.
4.
The effect of concentration of CDs (below 1.0wt%) on the drilling fluid rheology was negligible.
5.
0.3wt% CDs in drilling fluid was most favorable concentration for obtaining the maximum increase in induction time of hydrates.
From the above conclusions finally, it was concluded that ethylene glycol CDs can be effectively used in drilling fluid as a clathrate hydrate inhibitor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the Indian Institute of Technology (Indian school of Mines), Dhanbad, India for providing financial support and necessary laboratory facilities to carry out this research work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New gas hydrate inhibitors for deepwater drilling fluids
Proceedings of the IADC/SPE Drilling Conference
Global Long-term Mean Land and Sea Surface Temperatures
